articles.headline: Government Unveils Enhanced Incentives for Green Hydrogen Mission, Targets Global Leadership
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved a comprehensive package of incentives and policy reforms under the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
• Focus areas include research and development, manufacturing of electrolysers, and development of green hydrogen production hubs.
• The policy aims to reduce India's reliance on fossil fuels, achieve energy independence, and position India as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
• Key incentives include financial support for pilot projects, tax breaks for green hydrogen production units, and a simplified regulatory framework.
• This move is crucial for meeting India's net-zero targets and fostering a sustainable energy transition.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission: Launched in 2023.
Target: Production of 5 MMT (Million Metric Tonnes) of Green Hydrogen by 2030.
Financial Outlay: Approximately ₹19,744 crore.
Objectives: Decarbonisation, reducing fossil fuel imports, creating export opportunities, job creation.
Components: SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme, pilot projects, R&D, skill development.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the National Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security and climate commitments.
Analyze the potential economic benefits and challenges in establishing India as a global leader in green hydrogen production.
Evaluate the role of government policies and incentives in accelerating the adoption and production of green hydrogen.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of a green hydrogen production facility with wind turbines and solar panels in the background.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

articles.headline: India-Japan Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Emphasizes Deeper Indo-Pacific Cooperation and Defence Ties
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan Strategic Dialogue concluded in New Delhi, reaffirming commitment to a Free and Open Indo-Pacific.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defence cooperation, maritime security, economic partnerships, and collaboration in critical and emerging technologies.
• Both nations reiterated their support for multilateralism and a rules-based international order.
• Key outcomes included agreements on joint military exercises, technology transfer, and greater coordination in regional security frameworks.
• The dialogue is a cornerstone of the India-Japan Special Strategic and Global Partnership.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India-Japan relations: Elevated to 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership' in 2014.
Key pillars of cooperation: Defence, nuclear energy, high-speed rail, digital partnership.
Indo-Pacific: Shared vision for a region free from coercion, promoting connectivity and prosperity.
QUAD members: India, Japan, USA, Australia.
Joint exercises: Dharma Guardian (Army), Malabar (Naval - with US, Australia), JIMEX (Naval).
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic convergence between India and Japan in the context of the evolving Indo-Pacific geopolitical landscape.
Discuss how India-Japan cooperation can contribute to regional stability and economic prosperity.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in deepening defence and technological collaboration between the two nations.
articles.imageDescription: Delegates from India and Japan shaking hands during a formal dialogue.
articles.trivia: Japan is one of the largest investors in India, particularly in infrastructure projects like the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail corridor.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite for Enhanced Disaster Management
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched a new generation Earth Observation Satellite (EOS) from Sriharikota.
• The satellite is equipped with high-resolution imaging capabilities and advanced sensors designed for disaster monitoring, climate studies, and resource management.
• It will provide critical data for early warning systems for floods, cyclones, and other natural calamities, significantly improving India's disaster preparedness.
• The mission reinforces India's self-reliance in space technology and its commitment to utilizing space for societal benefit.
• This launch marks another milestone in ISRO's ambitious space exploration program.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: EOS series satellites: Used for various applications including agriculture, forestry, water resources, urban planning, and disaster support.
Sriharikota: Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) SHAR, India's primary spaceport.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) or GSLV (Geosynchronous Satellite Launch Vehicle): Primary launch vehicles of ISRO.
Applications: Flood mapping, cyclone tracking, drought assessment, forest fire detection, glacier monitoring.
Significance: Enhances India's capabilities in independent disaster risk reduction and environmental monitoring.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of space technology in disaster management and sustainable development in India.
Evaluate ISRO's contributions to national development through its various satellite programs.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities for India in leveraging geospatial data for effective governance and resource planning.
articles.imageDescription: A rocket launching into space from a launchpad with a trail of smoke.
articles.trivia: India's first satellite, Aryabhata, was launched in 1975 by the Soviet Union.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Issues Stringent Directives on Single-Use Plastic Ban Enforcement and Producer Responsibility
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, hearing a petition on environmental pollution, issued a series of directives to central and state governments regarding the enforcement of the single-use plastic (SUP) ban.
• The court emphasized the need for stricter implementation mechanisms, increased public awareness campaigns, and robust waste management infrastructure.
• It also directed a review of the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework to ensure accountability of manufacturers for plastic waste.
• The ruling aims to curb plastic pollution, which poses a significant threat to ecosystems and human health.
• This decision underscores the judiciary's role in environmental governance and policy implementation.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: India's SUP ban: Initiated in phases, with a comprehensive ban on specific items from July 1, 2022.
Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016: Amended in 2021 and 2022, introducing EPR guidelines.
EPR: A policy approach in which producers are given a significant financial and/or physical responsibility for the treatment or disposal of post-consumer products.
Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB): Nodal agency for implementing environmental regulations.
Impact of plastic pollution: Marine life, soil contamination, microplastics in food chain, climate change.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the effectiveness of the single-use plastic ban in India and suggest measures for improved implementation.
Discuss the challenges in waste management in India and the role of Extended Producer Responsibility in addressing plastic pollution.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in environmental protection and the enforcement of environmental laws in India.
articles.imageDescription: A broken plastic bottle lying on a polluted beach.
articles.trivia: It is estimated that over 8 million tonnes of plastic enter the oceans each year.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Interest Rates, Highlights Persistent Inflationary Pressures in Monetary Policy Report
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) announced its bi-monthly review, deciding to keep the repo rate unchanged.
• The MPC cited persistent inflationary pressures, particularly from food and energy prices, as the primary reason for maintaining the status quo.
• The report provided an updated outlook on economic growth, acknowledging global uncertainties but projecting a resilient domestic economy.
• RBI reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting growth.
• This decision signals a cautious approach to monetary policy amidst evolving economic conditions.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Effects of liberalization on the economy
prelimsPoints: Monetary Policy Committee (MPC): Six-member body, responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate in India.
Repo Rate: The rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation Target: RBI's mandate is to maintain retail inflation within 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Instruments of Monetary Policy: Repo rate, reverse repo rate, open market operations, CRR, SLR.
MPC meetings: Held at least four times a year.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in managing inflation while fostering economic growth in India.
Analyze the impact of global economic uncertainties on India's monetary policy decisions.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the Monetary Policy Committee in achieving its inflation targeting mandate.
articles.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai.
articles.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

articles.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Sweeping Reforms for Greater Transparency in Election Funding
articles.explainer:
• A parliamentary standing committee submitted its report on electoral reforms, with a significant focus on enhancing transparency in political funding.
• The panel recommended abolishing electoral bonds and introducing alternative mechanisms for donation disclosure and regulation.
• It also suggested capping individual and corporate donations to political parties and strengthening the Election Commission's oversight powers.
• The recommendations aim to address concerns about opaque funding, undue corporate influence, and the level playing field in elections.
• Implementation of these reforms could significantly impact India's democratic process.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: Electoral Bonds: Introduced in 2017, allowed individuals and corporations to donate to political parties anonymously.
Supreme Court ruling on Electoral Bonds: Declared unconstitutional in February 2024, citing violation of right to information.
Election Commission of India (ECI): Constitutional body responsible for conducting elections.
Representation of the People Act (RPA), 1951: Governs the conduct of elections.
Key issues in election funding: Black money, corporate influence, lack of transparency, expenditure limits.
mainsPoints: Discuss the arguments for and against electoral bonds as a mechanism for political funding in India.
Analyze the challenges in ensuring transparency and accountability in election funding and suggest comprehensive reforms.
Evaluate the role of parliamentary committees in shaping policy and governance in India.
articles.imageDescription: An illustration of a ballot box with currency notes flowing into it.
articles.trivia: India is the world's largest democracy, with over 900 million eligible voters in the last general election.

articles.headline: Union Government Launches 'Manas Mitra' Scheme for Mental Health Support in Rural Areas
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare launched 'Manas Mitra', a new national scheme aimed at providing accessible mental health support in rural and remote areas.
• The scheme focuses on training community health workers and volunteers to identify, counsel, and refer individuals experiencing mental health issues.
• It integrates mental health services with existing primary healthcare infrastructure, leveraging technology for tele-counselling and awareness campaigns.
• The initiative seeks to destigmatize mental illness and ensure early intervention, particularly among vulnerable populations.
• This is a significant step towards achieving universal health coverage with a focus on holistic well-being.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
prelimsPoints: National Mental Health Program (NMHP): Launched in 1982, aims to provide universal access to mental healthcare.
District Mental Health Program (DMHP): Aims to provide mental health services at the district level.
Ayushman Bharat: Aims for Universal Health Coverage, includes Health and Wellness Centres (HWCs) which are crucial for primary care.
Mental Healthcare Act, 2017: Protects rights of persons with mental illness, ensures access to care.
Challenge: Shortage of mental health professionals, stigma, lack of awareness, particularly in rural areas.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges in addressing mental health issues in rural India and how schemes like 'Manas Mitra' can bridge the gaps.
Analyze the importance of community participation and destigmatization in promoting mental well-being across the country.
Evaluate the role of technology in expanding access to mental healthcare services in underserved regions.
articles.imageDescription: A group of rural women participating in a community health workshop.
articles.trivia: According to WHO, mental health accounts for over 15% of the total disease burden in India.

articles.headline: BSF Deploys Advanced Surveillance Technology Along Western Border to Counter Infiltration and Smuggling
articles.explainer:
• The Border Security Force (BSF) has operationalized a new integrated surveillance system along critical stretches of India's western border.
• The system incorporates cutting-edge technologies such as drones, ground sensors, thermal imagers, and artificial intelligence-powered analytics.
• Its primary objective is to detect and deter cross-border infiltration, drug trafficking, and arms smuggling more effectively.
• This deployment is part of India's comprehensive border management strategy to enhance security and reduce human casualties.
• The move signifies a major upgrade in India's border defense capabilities.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: BSF: India's primary border guarding force for land borders, specifically with Pakistan and Bangladesh.
Western Border: Primarily with Pakistan, characterized by diverse terrain (desert, riverine, marshy).
CIBMS: A technological solution for border management, integrating various surveillance and communication devices.
Threats: Infiltration by terrorists, smuggling of drugs and weapons, cattle smuggling.
Indigenous technology: Emphasis on developing and deploying Indian-made surveillance systems.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multifaceted challenges of border management in India's western sector and how technology can provide effective solutions.
Analyze the concept of Comprehensive Integrated Border Management System (CIBMS) and its importance for national security.
Evaluate the ethical and privacy concerns associated with extensive surveillance technologies in border areas.
articles.imageDescription: A drone flying over a desert landscape with border fences visible in the distance.
articles.trivia: The BSF was raised in 1965 in the aftermath of the Indo-Pakistani War.

articles.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Uncovers Significant Findings at Ancient Site in Odisha
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of new artifacts and structural remains at an excavation site in Odisha.
• Preliminary findings suggest the site dates back to the early historic period, potentially revealing insights into ancient urban planning and trade routes.
• Excavations have unearthed pottery, tools, and remnants of what appears to be a sophisticated drainage system, indicating a developed civilization.
• This discovery is expected to shed new light on the cultural and economic history of ancient Kalinga (Odisha).
• The ASI plans further extensive research and conservation efforts at the site.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: ASI: Premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage in India, founded in 1861.
Kalinga: Ancient name for the coastal region of Odisha, known for its maritime trade and the Kalinga War.
Early Historic Period: Generally refers to the period from 6th century BCE to 4th century CE, marked by the rise of Mahajanapadas and early empires.
Significance of discoveries: Helps reconstruct past societies, understand cultural evolution, and economic activities.
Odisha's rich heritage: Home to numerous ancient sites, temples (Konark, Lingaraj), and Buddhist complexes (Udayagiri, Ratnagiri).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of archaeological discoveries in reconstructing India's ancient history and cultural evolution.
Analyze the challenges faced by the ASI in preserving and managing India's vast archaeological heritage.
Evaluate the contribution of regional kingdoms like Kalinga to India's cultural and economic landscape in the early historic period.
articles.imageDescription: Archaeologists carefully excavating an ancient site, revealing pottery fragments.
articles.trivia: The Konark Sun Temple in Odisha is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, renowned for its intricate stone carvings.

articles.headline: UN Report Warns of Worsening Global Food Security Crisis Amidst Escalating Climate Change Impacts
articles.explainer:
• A new report released by the United Nations highlighted a critical deterioration in global food security, exacerbated by climate change, conflicts, and economic instability.
• The report projects a significant increase in the number of people facing acute food insecurity and malnutrition in the coming decade.
• It calls for urgent international cooperation, sustainable agricultural practices, and climate adaptation measures to avert a humanitarian catastrophe.
• The findings underscore the interconnectedness of environmental, social, and economic factors influencing global food systems.
• India, as a major agricultural producer, faces both challenges and opportunities in contributing to global food security.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Issues relating to poverty and hunger
Food processing and related industries in India- scope and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.
prelimsPoints: SDG 2 (Zero Hunger): Aims to end hunger, achieve food security and improved nutrition and promote sustainable agriculture by 2030.
Drivers of food insecurity: Climate shocks (droughts, floods), conflicts, economic downturns, poverty, rising food prices.
Role of India: Major producer of food grains, significant role in global food supply, but also faces challenges of malnutrition and food waste.
UN agencies involved: FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization), WFP (World Food Programme), IFAD (International Fund for Agricultural Development).
Climate-resilient agriculture: Practices that help farming systems adapt to climate change and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
mainsPoints: Analyze the complex interplay between climate change, conflicts, and global food security, with specific reference to vulnerable regions.
Discuss India's role and responsibilities in addressing global food security challenges, considering its agricultural capacity and domestic nutritional issues.
Suggest comprehensive strategies, including policy interventions and technological innovations, to build climate-resilient food systems worldwide.
articles.imageDescription: A parched agricultural field with cracked earth, symbolizing drought.
articles.trivia: Globally, about one-third of all food produced for human consumption is lost or wasted.

articles.headline: New Section of Bharatmala Pariyojana Highway Inaugurated, Boosting Connectivity and Economic Activity
articles.explainer:
• A significant new section of the Bharatmala Pariyojana highway network was inaugurated, connecting key economic corridors.
• The project aims to reduce travel time, enhance logistics efficiency, and promote regional economic development.
• This infrastructure development is part of the government's broader vision to modernize India's road network and improve multimodal connectivity.
• The new highway section is expected to facilitate smoother movement of goods and people, benefiting trade and tourism.
• It underscores the government's commitment to robust infrastructure investment as a driver of economic growth.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models
prelimsPoints: Bharatmala Pariyojana: A centrally sponsored and funded road and highways project of the Government of India, launched in 2017.
Objectives: Optimize efficiency of freight and passenger movement, develop economic corridors, border roads, coastal roads, expressways.
Phases: Phase I involves construction of 34,800 km of roads by 2022 (extended targets).
Implementing agency: National Highways Authority of India (NHAI).
Impact: Reduced logistics costs, increased average speed of freight vehicles, improved safety.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of the Bharatmala Pariyojana in transforming India's logistics sector and fostering regional integration.
Analyze the economic and social benefits of large-scale infrastructure projects like national highways.
Examine the challenges in project implementation and funding for such mega infrastructure initiatives in India.
articles.imageDescription: A newly constructed multi-lane highway stretching into the distance with minimal traffic.
articles.trivia: India has the second-largest road network in the world, after the United States.

articles.headline: High Court Rules on Privacy Implications of Digital Health Records, Emphasizes Data Protection
articles.explainer:
• A High Court delivered a landmark judgment concerning the privacy implications of digital health records and the government's proposed digital health infrastructure.
• The ruling underscored the fundamental right to privacy and mandated stringent data protection measures for patient information.
• It directed the government to establish a robust legal framework and secure technological protocols before universal implementation of digital health IDs.
• The judgment addresses public concerns regarding data breaches, misuse of sensitive health information, and individual autonomy.
• This decision is crucial for balancing public health goals with individual privacy rights in the digital age.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM): Aims to develop the backbone necessary to support the integrated digital health infrastructure of the country.
Key components of ABDM: Health ID, Healthcare Professionals Registry (HPR), Health Facility Registry (HFR).
Justice K. S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) vs Union of India: Supreme Court judgment (2017) declared right to privacy as a fundamental right.
Data Protection Bill: India's proposed legislation to regulate processing of personal data.
Challenges: Data security, interoperability, digital literacy, consent management.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical and legal challenges in implementing a universal digital health records system in India, with particular emphasis on privacy concerns.
Analyze the balance between public health objectives and individual data privacy rights in the context of digital transformation.
Critically evaluate the existing and proposed legal frameworks for data protection in India and suggest improvements for sensitive health data.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image of a medical chart with digital lock icons, representing data security.
articles.trivia: India's Aadhaar system is the world's largest biometric ID program.

articles.headline: New Report Highlights Accelerated Himalayan Glacier Melt, Posing Threat to India's Water Security
articles.explainer:
• A new scientific report detailed an alarming rate of glacier melt in the Himalayas, attributing it primarily to global warming.
• The study warned of severe long-term consequences for the water security of millions of people dependent on Himalayan rivers.
• It projected increased instances of flash floods and water scarcity as glaciers retreat, impacting agriculture and hydropower generation.
• The report calls for urgent climate action and robust water resource management strategies in the region.
• This underscores the vulnerability of the Indian subcontinent to climate change impacts.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Conservation
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: Himalayan Glaciers: Often referred to as the 'Third Pole' due to the largest concentration of ice outside the polar regions.
Major rivers originating from Himalayas: Indus, Ganga, Brahmaputra, Yamuna, Sutlej.
Impact of melt: Initially increased river flow (flash floods), followed by reduced flow (water scarcity) in the long term.
Vulnerable sectors: Agriculture (irrigation), hydropower generation, drinking water supply.
Climate change indicators: Rising temperatures, altered precipitation patterns, extreme weather events.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted impacts of Himalayan glacier melt on India's water resources, agriculture, and energy sector.
Discuss the challenges in developing effective climate adaptation and mitigation strategies for the Himalayan region.
Propose policy interventions and international cooperation mechanisms to address the threat of climate change to cryospheric systems.
articles.imageDescription: A retreating glacier in the Himalayas, showing exposed rock and a meltwater lake.
articles.trivia: The Himalayas contain approximately 15,000 glaciers, which store about 12,000 cubic kilometers of fresh water.

articles.headline: Indian Scientists Achieve Breakthrough in Indigenous Vaccine Development for Neglected Tropical Disease
articles.explainer:
• Indian scientists announced a significant breakthrough in developing an indigenous vaccine candidate for a prevalent neglected tropical disease (NTD).
• The vaccine, developed through collaborative research between government institutions and private biotech firms, has shown promising results in pre-clinical trials.
• This achievement is crucial for public health, as NTDs disproportionately affect vulnerable populations in low-income settings and often lack effective treatments.
• The development highlights India's growing capabilities in pharmaceutical research and vaccine manufacturing.
• Further clinical trials are planned to fast-track its availability.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
prelimsPoints: Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs): A diverse group of communicable diseases that prevail in tropical and subtropical conditions, affecting over a billion people worldwide.
Examples of NTDs: Dengue, Chikungunya, Kala-azar, Lymphatic Filariasis, Leprosy, Rabies.
India's role: Significant burden of NTDs, but also a major hub for vaccine manufacturing.
Vaccine development stages: Research, pre-clinical testing, clinical trials (Phase I, II, III), regulatory approval, manufacturing, distribution.
Public-private partnership: Essential for accelerating research and development in health sector.
mainsPoints: Discuss the socio-economic impact of Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) on vulnerable populations in India and the importance of indigenous vaccine development.
Analyze India's strengths and challenges in becoming a global leader in pharmaceutical research and vaccine manufacturing.
Evaluate the role of government policies and collaborative models in fostering scientific breakthroughs in public health.
articles.imageDescription: A scientist in a lab coat holding a test tube, with a microscope in the background.
articles.trivia: India produces 60% of the world's vaccines and contributes 70% of WHO's essential medicines.

articles.headline: Government Launches 'E-Saksham' Initiative for Digital Literacy Among Tribal Communities
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tribal Affairs, in collaboration with the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology, launched 'E-Saksham', a new initiative to promote digital literacy among tribal communities.
• The program aims to equip tribal youth and women with essential digital skills, including internet usage, online banking, and digital governance services.
• It will leverage local infrastructure and community leaders to conduct training programs in tribal-dominated districts.
• The initiative seeks to bridge the digital divide, empower tribal populations, and enhance their access to education, employment, and government welfare schemes.
• This is a key step towards inclusive development and ensuring no community is left behind in the digital age.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
keyTerms: Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (PMGDISHA)
prelimsPoints: Digital India program: Flagship program of the Government of India with a vision to transform India into a digitally empowered society and knowledge economy.
PMGDISHA: A scheme to make 6 crore rural households digitally literate.
Tribal communities: Often face challenges of remoteness, lack of infrastructure, and limited access to education and technology.
Benefits of digital literacy: Access to online education, healthcare, banking, government services, and employment opportunities.
Ministry of Tribal Affairs: Nodal ministry for overall policy, planning, and coordination of programs for tribal development.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of digital literacy for the socio-economic empowerment of tribal communities in India.
Analyze the challenges in bridging the digital divide in remote and tribal areas and suggest effective strategies for program implementation.
Evaluate how digital inclusion can enhance the effectiveness of government welfare schemes and promote participatory governance among marginalized groups.
articles.imageDescription: Tribal women and youth learning to use tablets in a rural setting.
articles.trivia: India is home to the largest tribal population in the world, with over 700 recognized Scheduled Tribes.

articles.headline: Tribute to Syed Abid Ali: An All-Rounder's Legacy in Indian Cricket
articles.explainer:
• Syed Abid Ali, a former Indian cricketer, passed away at the age of 83.
• Known for his all-round abilities as a right-arm medium-fast bowler and a hard-hitting lower-order batsman.
• He was a key member of the Indian team during the late 1960s and early 1970s.
• Ali played 29 Tests and 5 One Day Internationals for India, memorable for his spirited performances.
• His contributions were vital in several historic victories for India, leaving a significant mark on the sport.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: Syed Abid Ali: Born in Hyderabad, played for Hyderabad in domestic cricket.
Debut: Test debut against Australia in 1967.
Notable performance: Scored 71 runs and took 6 wickets against Australia in 1967-68.
Role in Indian cricket: Known for his fighting spirit and contributions with both bat and ball.
Post-retirement: Coached several teams, including the Maldives national team.
articles.imageDescription: A black and white photo of Syed Abid Ali in his cricket whites.
articles.trivia: Syed Abid Ali was part of the Indian team that achieved its first-ever Test series victory in England in 1971.

articles.headline: K. K. Kochu, Prominent Malayalam Writer and Social Activist, Passes Away at 76
articles.explainer:
• K. K. Kochu, a renowned Malayalam writer, critic, and prominent social activist, passed away at the age of 76.
• He was a significant voice in Dalit literature and consistently advocated for the rights and upliftment of marginalized communities.
• Kochu's writings extensively explored themes of social justice, identity, and historical narratives from an subaltern perspective.
• His work played a crucial role in shaping contemporary Malayalam literary discourse and social thought.
• He leaves behind a legacy of intellectual activism and literary contributions.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
Social empowerment
prelimsPoints: K. K. Kochu: Known for his contributions to Dalit intellectual discourse in Kerala.
Malayalam literature: One of the Dravidian languages, rich literary tradition.
Dalit literature: Literary movement focusing on the experiences of Dalits (formerly untouchables), often characterized by protest and social commentary.
Social activism: Advocated for land rights, education, and political representation for marginalized groups.
Impact: Influenced a generation of writers and activists in Kerala.
articles.imageDescription: A portrait photo of K. K. Kochu.
articles.trivia: Kerala has the highest literacy rate among Indian states.

articles.headline: Lil Bahadur Chettri, Acclaimed Nepali-Indian Novelist, Passes Away at 92
articles.explainer:
• Noted Nepali-Indian novelist Lil Bahadur Chettri passed away at the age of 92.
• He was a towering figure in Nepali literature, celebrated for his realistic portrayal of life in the Nepali-speaking communities of India.
• His most famous work, 'Basain', is a classic of Nepali literature, depicting the struggles of rural life and migration.
• Chettri's contributions earned him numerous accolades, including the Sahitya Akademi Award.
• His literary legacy continues to inspire generations of writers and readers.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: Lil Bahadur Chettri: Born in Assam, wrote primarily in Nepali language.
Basain (The Migration): His seminal work, translated into several languages, depicting the plight of Nepali migrants.
Sahitya Akademi Award: One of India's highest literary honors, awarded to him for his contributions to Nepali literature.
Region: Associated with the Nepali-speaking communities in Northeast India (Assam, Sikkim, Darjeeling).
Theme: Often explored themes of identity, displacement, poverty, and social change.
articles.imageDescription: A formal portrait of Lil Bahadur Chettri.
articles.trivia: Nepali is one of the 22 scheduled languages of India.

articles.headline: Ramakanta Rath, Eminent Odia Modernist Poet, Passes Away at 90
articles.explainer:
• Ramakanta Rath, a towering figure in Odia literature and a distinguished modernist poet, passed away at the age of 90.
• He was celebrated for his profound philosophical insights, lyrical depth, and innovative poetic style.
• Rath's works explored themes of existentialism, human relationships, and the complexities of modern life, often drawing from ancient myths.
• A recipient of the Sahitya Akademi Award and the Padma Bhushan, his contributions significantly enriched Odia poetry.
• His demise marks the end of an era in Indian poetic tradition.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: Ramakanta Rath: A prominent figure in post-independence Odia literature.
Literary style: Known for his sophisticated imagery, intellectual rigor, and use of free verse.
Awards: Recipient of Sahitya Akademi Award (1977), Sarala Award, and Padma Bhushan (2006).
Themes: Often engaged with the human condition, alienation, and spiritual quests.
Significance: Helped redefine Odia poetry and brought it to national recognition.
articles.imageDescription: A black and white portrait photo of Ramakanta Rath.
articles.trivia: Odia is one of the six classical languages of India, granted the status in 2014.

highlights: Government announces enhanced incentives for National Green Hydrogen Mission, targeting global leadership in clean energy.
India-Japan Strategic Dialogue concludes, emphasizing deeper Indo-Pacific cooperation and defence ties.
ISRO successfully launches an advanced Earth Observation Satellite for enhanced disaster management and climate monitoring.
Supreme Court issues stringent directives on single-use plastic ban enforcement and Extended Producer Responsibility.
RBI maintains status quo on interest rates in its Monetary Policy Report, citing persistent inflationary pressures.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about the National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) is/are correct?
1. It aims to achieve a target of 5 Million Metric Tonnes (MMT) of Green Hydrogen production by 2030.
2. The mission focuses solely on the production aspect, with no provisions for research and development.
3. The SIGHT programme is a component of the NGHM, providing financial incentives for pilot projects.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The NGHM aims for 5 MMT of Green Hydrogen production by 2030. Statement 2 is incorrect. The mission focuses on R&D, manufacturing of electrolysers, and production hubs, among other aspects. Statement 3 is correct. SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme is a key component offering financial incentives.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following about India-Japan relations:
1. Both countries are members of the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD).
2. India and Japan have a 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership'.
3. JIMEX is a joint naval exercise conducted between India and Japan.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. India and Japan are indeed part of the QUAD, their bilateral relationship is designated as a 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership', and JIMEX is a regularly held joint naval exercise between the two nations.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Supreme Court's directives on single-use plastic (SUP) ban enforcement, consider the following:
1. The directives call for stricter implementation mechanisms and robust waste management infrastructure.
2. They also emphasize a review of the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework.
3. India's comprehensive ban on specific SUP items came into effect from July 1, 2022.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. The Supreme Court's directives indeed seek stricter implementation and EPR review. India's comprehensive ban on specific SUP items became effective from July 1, 2022.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following is NOT an instrument of monetary policy used by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI)?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Repo Rate, Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR), and Open Market Operations (OMO) are all instruments of monetary policy used by the RBI. Fiscal Deficit, on the other hand, relates to the government's fiscal policy, not monetary policy.

prelimsMocks.question: The 'Third Pole' is a term often used to refer to which of the following regions due to its large concentration of ice outside the polar regions?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Himalayan glaciers are often referred to as the 'Third Pole' because they hold the largest concentration of ice and snow outside of the Arctic and Antarctic regions, serving as a critical water source for billions of people.

mainsMocks.question: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission is a critical step towards achieving energy independence and climate goals. Discuss the potential of green hydrogen as a future energy source for India and the challenges in its large-scale adoption. (150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Energy; Environmental pollution and degradation; Conservation; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce Green Hydrogen and NGHM. Discuss potential (decarbonization, energy security, export, industrial use). Discuss challenges (cost of production, infrastructure, storage, R&D, water availability). Conclude with policy support.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's recent directives on single-use plastic ban enforcement highlight the persistent challenges in environmental governance in India. Analyze the effectiveness of India's plastic waste management policies and suggest measures for strengthening their implementation. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Environmental pollution and degradation, conservation; Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
mainsMocks.approach: Briefly mention SUP ban and SC directives. Analyze current policy effectiveness (successes, gaps in implementation, challenges like informal sector, public compliance). Suggest measures (stricter enforcement, awareness, tech solutions, EPR, circular economy, alternatives, inter-state cooperation).

mainsMocks.question: Discuss the multifaceted impacts of Himalayan glacier melt on India's water security, agriculture, and energy sector. What policy interventions and international cooperation mechanisms are needed to address this existential threat? (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment; Conservation; Disaster and disaster management. GS1: Important Geophysical Phenomena.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce Himalayan glaciers as 'Third Pole' and the issue of accelerated melt. Detail impacts: water scarcity/floods, agriculture (irrigation), hydropower, ecosystem. Policy interventions: water management (storage, efficiency), early warning systems, climate-resilient agriculture, R&D. International cooperation: regional agreements, global climate action, research sharing. Conclude on urgency.

quickBytes.text: The National Green Hydrogen Mission aims for 5 MMT of Green Hydrogen production by 2030.
India and Japan share a 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership'.
ISRO's Earth Observation Satellites (EOS) are crucial for disaster monitoring and resource management.
Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) holds manufacturers accountable for post-consumer waste.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) sets India's benchmark interest rate (repo rate).
Syed Abid Ali, former Indian cricketer, known for his all-round abilities, passed away at 83.
K. K. Kochu was a prominent Malayalam writer and social activist, voice for Dalit literature.
Lil Bahadur Chettri, acclaimed Nepali-Indian novelist, authored the classic 'Basain'.
Ramakanta Rath, eminent Odia modernist poet, was a recipient of the Padma Bhushan.
The Bharatmala Pariyojana aims to optimize freight and passenger movement through highway development.

GS1.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Uncovers Significant Findings at Ancient Site in Odisha
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of new artifacts and structural remains at an excavation site in Odisha.
• Preliminary findings suggest the site dates back to the early historic period, potentially revealing insights into ancient urban planning and trade routes.
• Excavations have unearthed pottery, tools, and remnants of what appears to be a sophisticated drainage system, indicating a developed civilization.
• This discovery is expected to shed new light on the cultural and economic history of ancient Kalinga (Odisha).
• The ASI plans further extensive research and conservation efforts at the site.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: ASI: Premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage in India, founded in 1861.
Kalinga: Ancient name for the coastal region of Odisha, known for its maritime trade and the Kalinga War.
Early Historic Period: Generally refers to the period from 6th century BCE to 4th century CE, marked by the rise of Mahajanapadas and early empires.
Significance of discoveries: Helps reconstruct past societies, understand cultural evolution, and economic activities.
Odisha's rich heritage: Home to numerous ancient sites, temples (Konark, Lingaraj), and Buddhist complexes (Udayagiri, Ratnagiri).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of archaeological discoveries in reconstructing India's ancient history and cultural evolution.
Analyze the challenges faced by the ASI in preserving and managing India's vast archaeological heritage.
Evaluate the contribution of regional kingdoms like Kalinga to India's cultural and economic landscape in the early historic period.
GS1.imageDescription: Archaeologists carefully excavating an ancient site, revealing pottery fragments.
GS1.trivia: The Konark Sun Temple in Odisha is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, renowned for its intricate stone carvings.

GS1.headline: Tribute to Syed Abid Ali: An All-Rounder's Legacy in Indian Cricket
GS1.explainer:
• Syed Abid Ali, a former Indian cricketer, passed away at the age of 83.
• Known for his all-round abilities as a right-arm medium-fast bowler and a hard-hitting lower-order batsman.
• He was a key member of the Indian team during the late 1960s and early 1970s.
• Ali played 29 Tests and 5 One Day Internationals for India, memorable for his spirited performances.
• His contributions were vital in several historic victories for India, leaving a significant mark on the sport.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: Syed Abid Ali: Born in Hyderabad, played for Hyderabad in domestic cricket.
Debut: Test debut against Australia in 1967.
Notable performance: Scored 71 runs and took 6 wickets against Australia in 1967-68.
Role in Indian cricket: Known for his fighting spirit and contributions with both bat and ball.
Post-retirement: Coached several teams, including the Maldives national team.
GS1.imageDescription: A black and white photo of Syed Abid Ali in his cricket whites.
GS1.trivia: Syed Abid Ali was part of the Indian team that achieved its first-ever Test series victory in England in 1971.

GS1.headline: K. K. Kochu, Prominent Malayalam Writer and Social Activist, Passes Away at 76
GS1.explainer:
• K. K. Kochu, a renowned Malayalam writer, critic, and prominent social activist, passed away at the age of 76.
• He was a significant voice in Dalit literature and consistently advocated for the rights and upliftment of marginalized communities.
• Kochu's writings extensively explored themes of social justice, identity, and historical narratives from an subaltern perspective.
• His work played a crucial role in shaping contemporary Malayalam literary discourse and social thought.
• He leaves behind a legacy of intellectual activism and literary contributions.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
Social empowerment
prelimsPoints: K. K. Kochu: Known for his contributions to Dalit intellectual discourse in Kerala.
Malayalam literature: One of the Dravidian languages, rich literary tradition.
Dalit literature: Literary movement focusing on the experiences of Dalits (formerly untouchables), often characterized by protest and social commentary.
Social activism: Advocated for land rights, education, and political representation for marginalized groups.
Impact: Influenced a generation of writers and activists in Kerala.
GS1.imageDescription: A portrait photo of K. K. Kochu.
GS1.trivia: Kerala has the highest literacy rate among Indian states.

GS1.headline: Lil Bahadur Chettri, Acclaimed Nepali-Indian Novelist, Passes Away at 92
GS1.explainer:
• Noted Nepali-Indian novelist Lil Bahadur Chettri passed away at the age of 92.
• He was a towering figure in Nepali literature, celebrated for his realistic portrayal of life in the Nepali-speaking communities of India.
• His most famous work, 'Basain', is a classic of Nepali literature, depicting the struggles of rural life and migration.
• Chettri's contributions earned him numerous accolades, including the Sahitya Akademi Award.
• His literary legacy continues to inspire generations of writers and readers.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: Lil Bahadur Chettri: Born in Assam, wrote primarily in Nepali language.
Basain (The Migration): His seminal work, translated into several languages, depicting the plight of Nepali migrants.
Sahitya Akademi Award: One of India's highest literary honors, awarded to him for his contributions to Nepali literature.
Region: Associated with the Nepali-speaking communities in Northeast India (Assam, Sikkim, Darjeeling).
Theme: Often explored themes of identity, displacement, poverty, and social change.
GS1.imageDescription: A formal portrait of Lil Bahadur Chettri.
GS1.trivia: Nepali is one of the 22 scheduled languages of India.

GS1.headline: Ramakanta Rath, Eminent Odia Modernist Poet, Passes Away at 90
GS1.explainer:
• Ramakanta Rath, a towering figure in Odia literature and a distinguished modernist poet, passed away at the age of 90.
• He was celebrated for his profound philosophical insights, lyrical depth, and innovative poetic style.
• Rath's works explored themes of existentialism, human relationships, and the complexities of modern life, often drawing from ancient myths.
• A recipient of the Sahitya Akademi Award and the Padma Bhushan, his contributions significantly enriched Odia poetry.
• His demise marks the end of an era in Indian poetic tradition.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
prelimsPoints: Ramakanta Rath: A prominent figure in post-independence Odia literature.
Literary style: Known for his sophisticated imagery, intellectual rigor, and use of free verse.
Awards: Recipient of Sahitya Akademi Award (1977), Sarala Award, and Padma Bhushan (2006).
Themes: Often engaged with the human condition, alienation, and spiritual quests.
Significance: Helped redefine Odia poetry and brought it to national recognition.
GS1.imageDescription: A black and white portrait photo of Ramakanta Rath.
GS1.trivia: Odia is one of the six classical languages of India, granted the status in 2014.

GS2.headline: India-Japan Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Emphasizes Deeper Indo-Pacific Cooperation and Defence Ties
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan Strategic Dialogue concluded in New Delhi, reaffirming commitment to a Free and Open Indo-Pacific.
• Discussions focused on enhancing defence cooperation, maritime security, economic partnerships, and collaboration in critical and emerging technologies.
• Both nations reiterated their support for multilateralism and a rules-based international order.
• Key outcomes included agreements on joint military exercises, technology transfer, and greater coordination in regional security frameworks.
• The dialogue is a cornerstone of the India-Japan Special Strategic and Global Partnership.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India-Japan relations: Elevated to 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership' in 2014.
Key pillars of cooperation: Defence, nuclear energy, high-speed rail, digital partnership.
Indo-Pacific: Shared vision for a region free from coercion, promoting connectivity and prosperity.
QUAD members: India, Japan, USA, Australia.
Joint exercises: Dharma Guardian (Army), Malabar (Naval - with US, Australia), JIMEX (Naval).
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic convergence between India and Japan in the context of the evolving Indo-Pacific geopolitical landscape.
Discuss how India-Japan cooperation can contribute to regional stability and economic prosperity.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in deepening defence and technological collaboration between the two nations.
GS2.imageDescription: Delegates from India and Japan shaking hands during a formal dialogue.
GS2.trivia: Japan is one of the largest investors in India, particularly in infrastructure projects like the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail corridor.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Sweeping Reforms for Greater Transparency in Election Funding
GS2.explainer:
• A parliamentary standing committee submitted its report on electoral reforms, with a significant focus on enhancing transparency in political funding.
• The panel recommended abolishing electoral bonds and introducing alternative mechanisms for donation disclosure and regulation.
• It also suggested capping individual and corporate donations to political parties and strengthening the Election Commission's oversight powers.
• The recommendations aim to address concerns about opaque funding, undue corporate influence, and the level playing field in elections.
• Implementation of these reforms could significantly impact India's democratic process.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: Electoral Bonds: Introduced in 2017, allowed individuals and corporations to donate to political parties anonymously.
Supreme Court ruling on Electoral Bonds: Declared unconstitutional in February 2024, citing violation of right to information.
Election Commission of India (ECI): Constitutional body responsible for conducting elections.
Representation of the People Act (RPA), 1951: Governs the conduct of elections.
Key issues in election funding: Black money, corporate influence, lack of transparency, expenditure limits.
mainsPoints: Discuss the arguments for and against electoral bonds as a mechanism for political funding in India.
Analyze the challenges in ensuring transparency and accountability in election funding and suggest comprehensive reforms.
Evaluate the role of parliamentary committees in shaping policy and governance in India.
GS2.imageDescription: An illustration of a ballot box with currency notes flowing into it.
GS2.trivia: India is the world's largest democracy, with over 900 million eligible voters in the last general election.

GS2.headline: Union Government Launches 'Manas Mitra' Scheme for Mental Health Support in Rural Areas
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare launched 'Manas Mitra', a new national scheme aimed at providing accessible mental health support in rural and remote areas.
• The scheme focuses on training community health workers and volunteers to identify, counsel, and refer individuals experiencing mental health issues.
• It integrates mental health services with existing primary healthcare infrastructure, leveraging technology for tele-counselling and awareness campaigns.
• The initiative seeks to destigmatize mental illness and ensure early intervention, particularly among vulnerable populations.
• This is a significant step towards achieving universal health coverage with a focus on holistic well-being.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
prelimsPoints: National Mental Health Program (NMHP): Launched in 1982, aims to provide universal access to mental healthcare.
District Mental Health Program (DMHP): Aims to provide mental health services at the district level.
Ayushman Bharat: Aims for Universal Health Coverage, includes Health and Wellness Centres (HWCs) which are crucial for primary care.
Mental Healthcare Act, 2017: Protects rights of persons with mental illness, ensures access to care.
Challenge: Shortage of mental health professionals, stigma, lack of awareness, particularly in rural areas.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges in addressing mental health issues in rural India and how schemes like 'Manas Mitra' can bridge the gaps.
Analyze the importance of community participation and destigmatization in promoting mental well-being across the country.
Evaluate the role of technology in expanding access to mental healthcare services in underserved regions.
GS2.imageDescription: A group of rural women participating in a community health workshop.
GS2.trivia: According to WHO, mental health accounts for over 15% of the total disease burden in India.

GS2.headline: UN Report Warns of Worsening Global Food Security Crisis Amidst Escalating Climate Change Impacts
GS2.explainer:
• A new report released by the United Nations highlighted a critical deterioration in global food security, exacerbated by climate change, conflicts, and economic instability.
• The report projects a significant increase in the number of people facing acute food insecurity and malnutrition in the coming decade.
• It calls for urgent international cooperation, sustainable agricultural practices, and climate adaptation measures to avert a humanitarian catastrophe.
• The findings underscore the interconnectedness of environmental, social, and economic factors influencing global food systems.
• India, as a major agricultural producer, faces both challenges and opportunities in contributing to global food security.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Issues relating to poverty and hunger
Food processing and related industries in India- scope and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.
prelimsPoints: SDG 2 (Zero Hunger): Aims to end hunger, achieve food security and improved nutrition and promote sustainable agriculture by 2030.
Drivers of food insecurity: Climate shocks (droughts, floods), conflicts, economic downturns, poverty, rising food prices.
Role of India: Major producer of food grains, significant role in global food supply, but also faces challenges of malnutrition and food waste.
UN agencies involved: FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization), WFP (World Food Programme), IFAD (International Fund for Agricultural Development).
Climate-resilient agriculture: Practices that help farming systems adapt to climate change and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
mainsPoints: Analyze the complex interplay between climate change, conflicts, and global food security, with specific reference to vulnerable regions.
Discuss India's role and responsibilities in addressing global food security challenges, considering its agricultural capacity and domestic nutritional issues.
Suggest comprehensive strategies, including policy interventions and technological innovations, to build climate-resilient food systems worldwide.
GS2.imageDescription: A parched agricultural field with cracked earth, symbolizing drought.
GS2.trivia: Globally, about one-third of all food produced for human consumption is lost or wasted.

GS2.headline: High Court Rules on Privacy Implications of Digital Health Records, Emphasizes Data Protection
GS2.explainer:
• A High Court delivered a landmark judgment concerning the privacy implications of digital health records and the government's proposed digital health infrastructure.
• The ruling underscored the fundamental right to privacy and mandated stringent data protection measures for patient information.
• It directed the government to establish a robust legal framework and secure technological protocols before universal implementation of digital health IDs.
• The judgment addresses public concerns regarding data breaches, misuse of sensitive health information, and individual autonomy.
• This decision is crucial for balancing public health goals with individual privacy rights in the digital age.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM): Aims to develop the backbone necessary to support the integrated digital health infrastructure of the country.
Key components of ABDM: Health ID, Healthcare Professionals Registry (HPR), Health Facility Registry (HFR).
Justice K. S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) vs Union of India: Supreme Court judgment (2017) declared right to privacy as a fundamental right.
Data Protection Bill: India's proposed legislation to regulate processing of personal data.
Challenges: Data security, interoperability, digital literacy, consent management.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical and legal challenges in implementing a universal digital health records system in India, with particular emphasis on privacy concerns.
Analyze the balance between public health objectives and individual data privacy rights in the context of digital transformation.
Critically evaluate the existing and proposed legal frameworks for data protection in India and suggest improvements for sensitive health data.
GS2.imageDescription: A stylized image of a medical chart with digital lock icons, representing data security.
GS2.trivia: India's Aadhaar system is the world's largest biometric ID program.

GS2.headline: Government Launches 'E-Saksham' Initiative for Digital Literacy Among Tribal Communities
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tribal Affairs, in collaboration with the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology, launched 'E-Saksham', a new initiative to promote digital literacy among tribal communities.
• The program aims to equip tribal youth and women with essential digital skills, including internet usage, online banking, and digital governance services.
• It will leverage local infrastructure and community leaders to conduct training programs in tribal-dominated districts.
• The initiative seeks to bridge the digital divide, empower tribal populations, and enhance their access to education, employment, and government welfare schemes.
• This is a key step towards inclusive development and ensuring no community is left behind in the digital age.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
keyTerms: Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (PMGDISHA)
prelimsPoints: Digital India program: Flagship program of the Government of India with a vision to transform India into a digitally empowered society and knowledge economy.
PMGDISHA: A scheme to make 6 crore rural households digitally literate.
Tribal communities: Often face challenges of remoteness, lack of infrastructure, and limited access to education and technology.
Benefits of digital literacy: Access to online education, healthcare, banking, government services, and employment opportunities.
Ministry of Tribal Affairs: Nodal ministry for overall policy, planning, and coordination of programs for tribal development.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of digital literacy for the socio-economic empowerment of tribal communities in India.
Analyze the challenges in bridging the digital divide in remote and tribal areas and suggest effective strategies for program implementation.
Evaluate how digital inclusion can enhance the effectiveness of government welfare schemes and promote participatory governance among marginalized groups.
GS2.imageDescription: Tribal women and youth learning to use tablets in a rural setting.
GS2.trivia: India is home to the largest tribal population in the world, with over 700 recognized Scheduled Tribes.

GS3.headline: Government Unveils Enhanced Incentives for Green Hydrogen Mission, Targets Global Leadership
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved a comprehensive package of incentives and policy reforms under the National Green Hydrogen Mission.
• Focus areas include research and development, manufacturing of electrolysers, and development of green hydrogen production hubs.
• The policy aims to reduce India's reliance on fossil fuels, achieve energy independence, and position India as a global hub for green hydrogen production and export.
• Key incentives include financial support for pilot projects, tax breaks for green hydrogen production units, and a simplified regulatory framework.
• This move is crucial for meeting India's net-zero targets and fostering a sustainable energy transition.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission: Launched in 2023.
Target: Production of 5 MMT (Million Metric Tonnes) of Green Hydrogen by 2030.
Financial Outlay: Approximately ₹19,744 crore.
Objectives: Decarbonisation, reducing fossil fuel imports, creating export opportunities, job creation.
Components: SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme, pilot projects, R&D, skill development.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the National Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security and climate commitments.
Analyze the potential economic benefits and challenges in establishing India as a global leader in green hydrogen production.
Evaluate the role of government policies and incentives in accelerating the adoption and production of green hydrogen.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of a green hydrogen production facility with wind turbines and solar panels in the background.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite for Enhanced Disaster Management
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched a new generation Earth Observation Satellite (EOS) from Sriharikota.
• The satellite is equipped with high-resolution imaging capabilities and advanced sensors designed for disaster monitoring, climate studies, and resource management.
• It will provide critical data for early warning systems for floods, cyclones, and other natural calamities, significantly improving India's disaster preparedness.
• The mission reinforces India's self-reliance in space technology and its commitment to utilizing space for societal benefit.
• This launch marks another milestone in ISRO's ambitious space exploration program.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: EOS series satellites: Used for various applications including agriculture, forestry, water resources, urban planning, and disaster support.
Sriharikota: Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) SHAR, India's primary spaceport.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) or GSLV (Geosynchronous Satellite Launch Vehicle): Primary launch vehicles of ISRO.
Applications: Flood mapping, cyclone tracking, drought assessment, forest fire detection, glacier monitoring.
Significance: Enhances India's capabilities in independent disaster risk reduction and environmental monitoring.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of space technology in disaster management and sustainable development in India.
Evaluate ISRO's contributions to national development through its various satellite programs.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities for India in leveraging geospatial data for effective governance and resource planning.
GS3.imageDescription: A rocket launching into space from a launchpad with a trail of smoke.
GS3.trivia: India's first satellite, Aryabhata, was launched in 1975 by the Soviet Union.

GS3.headline: Supreme Court Issues Stringent Directives on Single-Use Plastic Ban Enforcement and Producer Responsibility
GS3.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, hearing a petition on environmental pollution, issued a series of directives to central and state governments regarding the enforcement of the single-use plastic (SUP) ban.
• The court emphasized the need for stricter implementation mechanisms, increased public awareness campaigns, and robust waste management infrastructure.
• It also directed a review of the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework to ensure accountability of manufacturers for plastic waste.
• The ruling aims to curb plastic pollution, which poses a significant threat to ecosystems and human health.
• This decision underscores the judiciary's role in environmental governance and policy implementation.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: India's SUP ban: Initiated in phases, with a comprehensive ban on specific items from July 1, 2022.
Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016: Amended in 2021 and 2022, introducing EPR guidelines.
EPR: A policy approach in which producers are given a significant financial and/or physical responsibility for the treatment or disposal of post-consumer products.
Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB): Nodal agency for implementing environmental regulations.
Impact of plastic pollution: Marine life, soil contamination, microplastics in food chain, climate change.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the effectiveness of the single-use plastic ban in India and suggest measures for improved implementation.
Discuss the challenges in waste management in India and the role of Extended Producer Responsibility in addressing plastic pollution.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in environmental protection and the enforcement of environmental laws in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A broken plastic bottle lying on a polluted beach.
GS3.trivia: It is estimated that over 8 million tonnes of plastic enter the oceans each year.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Interest Rates, Highlights Persistent Inflationary Pressures in Monetary Policy Report
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) announced its bi-monthly review, deciding to keep the repo rate unchanged.
• The MPC cited persistent inflationary pressures, particularly from food and energy prices, as the primary reason for maintaining the status quo.
• The report provided an updated outlook on economic growth, acknowledging global uncertainties but projecting a resilient domestic economy.
• RBI reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting growth.
• This decision signals a cautious approach to monetary policy amidst evolving economic conditions.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Effects of liberalization on the economy
prelimsPoints: Monetary Policy Committee (MPC): Six-member body, responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate in India.
Repo Rate: The rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
Inflation Target: RBI's mandate is to maintain retail inflation within 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Instruments of Monetary Policy: Repo rate, reverse repo rate, open market operations, CRR, SLR.
MPC meetings: Held at least four times a year.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in managing inflation while fostering economic growth in India.
Analyze the impact of global economic uncertainties on India's monetary policy decisions.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the Monetary Policy Committee in achieving its inflation targeting mandate.
GS3.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai.
GS3.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, under the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

GS3.headline: BSF Deploys Advanced Surveillance Technology Along Western Border to Counter Infiltration and Smuggling
GS3.explainer:
• The Border Security Force (BSF) has operationalized a new integrated surveillance system along critical stretches of India's western border.
• The system incorporates cutting-edge technologies such as drones, ground sensors, thermal imagers, and artificial intelligence-powered analytics.
• Its primary objective is to detect and deter cross-border infiltration, drug trafficking, and arms smuggling more effectively.
• This deployment is part of India's comprehensive border management strategy to enhance security and reduce human casualties.
• The move signifies a major upgrade in India's border defense capabilities.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas
Linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: BSF: India's primary border guarding force for land borders, specifically with Pakistan and Bangladesh.
Western Border: Primarily with Pakistan, characterized by diverse terrain (desert, riverine, marshy).
CIBMS: A technological solution for border management, integrating various surveillance and communication devices.
Threats: Infiltration by terrorists, smuggling of drugs and weapons, cattle smuggling.
Indigenous technology: Emphasis on developing and deploying Indian-made surveillance systems.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multifaceted challenges of border management in India's western sector and how technology can provide effective solutions.
Analyze the concept of Comprehensive Integrated Border Management System (CIBMS) and its importance for national security.
Evaluate the ethical and privacy concerns associated with extensive surveillance technologies in border areas.
GS3.imageDescription: A drone flying over a desert landscape with border fences visible in the distance.
GS3.trivia: The BSF was raised in 1965 in the aftermath of the Indo-Pakistani War.

GS3.headline: New Section of Bharatmala Pariyojana Highway Inaugurated, Boosting Connectivity and Economic Activity
GS3.explainer:
• A significant new section of the Bharatmala Pariyojana highway network was inaugurated, connecting key economic corridors.
• The project aims to reduce travel time, enhance logistics efficiency, and promote regional economic development.
• This infrastructure development is part of the government's broader vision to modernize India's road network and improve multimodal connectivity.
• The new highway section is expected to facilitate smoother movement of goods and people, benefiting trade and tourism.
• It underscores the government's commitment to robust infrastructure investment as a driver of economic growth.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models
prelimsPoints: Bharatmala Pariyojana: A centrally sponsored and funded road and highways project of the Government of India, launched in 2017.
Objectives: Optimize efficiency of freight and passenger movement, develop economic corridors, border roads, coastal roads, expressways.
Phases: Phase I involves construction of 34,800 km of roads by 2022 (extended targets).
Implementing agency: National Highways Authority of India (NHAI).
Impact: Reduced logistics costs, increased average speed of freight vehicles, improved safety.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of the Bharatmala Pariyojana in transforming India's logistics sector and fostering regional integration.
Analyze the economic and social benefits of large-scale infrastructure projects like national highways.
Examine the challenges in project implementation and funding for such mega infrastructure initiatives in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A newly constructed multi-lane highway stretching into the distance with minimal traffic.
GS3.trivia: India has the second-largest road network in the world, after the United States.

GS3.headline: New Report Highlights Accelerated Himalayan Glacier Melt, Posing Threat to India's Water Security
GS3.explainer:
• A new scientific report detailed an alarming rate of glacier melt in the Himalayas, attributing it primarily to global warming.
• The study warned of severe long-term consequences for the water security of millions of people dependent on Himalayan rivers.
• It projected increased instances of flash floods and water scarcity as glaciers retreat, impacting agriculture and hydropower generation.
• The report calls for urgent climate action and robust water resource management strategies in the region.
• This underscores the vulnerability of the Indian subcontinent to climate change impacts.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Conservation
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: Himalayan Glaciers: Often referred to as the 'Third Pole' due to the largest concentration of ice outside the polar regions.
Major rivers originating from Himalayas: Indus, Ganga, Brahmaputra, Yamuna, Sutlej.
Impact of melt: Initially increased river flow (flash floods), followed by reduced flow (water scarcity) in the long term.
Vulnerable sectors: Agriculture (irrigation), hydropower generation, drinking water supply.
Climate change indicators: Rising temperatures, altered precipitation patterns, extreme weather events.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted impacts of Himalayan glacier melt on India's water resources, agriculture, and energy sector.
Discuss the challenges in developing effective climate adaptation and mitigation strategies for the Himalayan region.
Propose policy interventions and international cooperation mechanisms to address the threat of climate change to cryospheric systems.
GS3.imageDescription: A retreating glacier in the Himalayas, showing exposed rock and a meltwater lake.
GS3.trivia: The Himalayas contain approximately 15,000 glaciers, which store about 12,000 cubic kilometers of fresh water.

GS3.headline: Indian Scientists Achieve Breakthrough in Indigenous Vaccine Development for Neglected Tropical Disease
GS3.explainer:
• Indian scientists announced a significant breakthrough in developing an indigenous vaccine candidate for a prevalent neglected tropical disease (NTD).
• The vaccine, developed through collaborative research between government institutions and private biotech firms, has shown promising results in pre-clinical trials.
• This achievement is crucial for public health, as NTDs disproportionately affect vulnerable populations in low-income settings and often lack effective treatments.
• The development highlights India's growing capabilities in pharmaceutical research and vaccine manufacturing.
• Further clinical trials are planned to fast-track its availability.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
prelimsPoints: Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs): A diverse group of communicable diseases that prevail in tropical and subtropical conditions, affecting over a billion people worldwide.
Examples of NTDs: Dengue, Chikungunya, Kala-azar, Lymphatic Filariasis, Leprosy, Rabies.
India's role: Significant burden of NTDs, but also a major hub for vaccine manufacturing.
Vaccine development stages: Research, pre-clinical testing, clinical trials (Phase I, II, III), regulatory approval, manufacturing, distribution.
Public-private partnership: Essential for accelerating research and development in health sector.
mainsPoints: Discuss the socio-economic impact of Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) on vulnerable populations in India and the importance of indigenous vaccine development.
Analyze India's strengths and challenges in becoming a global leader in pharmaceutical research and vaccine manufacturing.
Evaluate the role of government policies and collaborative models in fostering scientific breakthroughs in public health.
GS3.imageDescription: A scientist in a lab coat holding a test tube, with a microscope in the background.
GS3.trivia: India produces 60% of the world's vaccines and contributes 70% of WHO's essential medicines.
